title: Replace BabyPbk with EscalarMulFix in HumanUniqueness for ~40% constraint reduction
description: HumanUniqueness uses BabyPbk() which internally does a full scalar multiplication on Baby Jubjub. Since the base point is always the generator (fixed), replacing it with EscalarMulFix (fixed-base scalar mul) cuts the scalar mul constraints from ~2000 to ~1200. This is the single largest constraint contributor after the Merkle proof and yields a measurable proving time improvement for the most latency-sensitive circuit in the handshake flow.

title: Extract shared credential commitment template to avoid AgentPolicy/Delegation/MIB divergence
description: AgentPolicy, Delegation, and ModelInstanceBinding each independently compute credentialCommitment = Poseidon5(modelHash, opPkAx, opPkAy, permissionBitmask, expiryTimestamp). Delegation also takes delegatorCredCommitment as private input but never verifies it matches this formula — it trusts the prover. Extract a shared CredentialCommitment(5) template and have Delegation recompute delegatorCredCommitment from its constituent fields, then constrain equality. This closes a soundness gap where a malicious prover can supply an arbitrary delegatorCredCommitment that doesn't correspond to any real credential.

title: Reduce provider registry Merkle depth from 8 to 5 in ModelInstanceBinding
description: ModelInstanceBinding uses PROVIDER_DEPTH=8 (256 providers) but the realistic provider count for the next 2+ years is under 32. Reducing to depth 5 saves ~900 constraints (3 fewer Poseidon2 hashes × ~300 each) and cuts MIB proving time by ~3%. The depth is a compile-time parameter so upgrading later just requires recompilation and new ceremony. Add a compile-time assertion or documented constant so the depth decision is explicit.

title: Switch AgentPolicy CI default from Groth16 to PLONK and benchmark end-to-end
description: AgentPolicy ships dual .zkey artifacts (Groth16 + PLONK) but tests and SDK default to Groth16. PLONK verification is ~2x more gas on-chain but eliminates the per-circuit trusted setup ceremony, which is the main deployment bottleneck for circuit upgrades. Run the existing bench_rapidsnark.js suite for both systems, record gas + proving time in a comparison table, and if PLONK proving stays under 3s with rapidsnark, make it the SDK default for AgentPolicy. This de-risks the ceremony dependency before mainnet.

title: Deduplicate Num2Bits range checks when same signal is checked in multiple subcircuits
description: AgentPolicy range-checks currentTimestamp (Num2Bits(64) = 64 constraints) and then the LessThan comparator for expiry internally decomposes both operands again. The double decomposition wastes ~64 constraints. Refactor to decompose each uint64 signal exactly once, then feed the bits array to both the range gate and the LessThan. Apply the same pattern in Delegation (delegateeExpiry and delegatorExpiry are each decomposed twice). Net saving: ~128 constraints per circuit, small but free.

title: Off-chain session token for stateless agent frameworks
description: LangChain/CrewAI agents are stateless across tool calls — they don't hold persistent connections. After a successful handshake, the SDK should emit a compact JWT-like session token (containing nullifierHash, scopeCommitment, expiry, and a HMAC over the sessionNonce) that agents can pass as a bearer credential on subsequent calls without re-proving. The IdentityRegistry needs a `verifySessionToken(bytes)` view function that checks the token against on-chain state (root freshness, nullifier non-revocation) without requiring a new ZK proof. This eliminates the 2-5 second proving overhead on every tool invocation in a multi-step agent chain.

title: CrewAI @delegated_task decorator with scope narrowing
description: CrewAI's task delegation model maps naturally to Bolyra's Delegation circuit, but the integration is missing. Build a `@delegated_task(max_scope=0b00000111)` Python decorator that wraps a CrewAI Task. When a crew manager delegates to a worker agent, the decorator automatically generates a Delegation proof narrowing the manager's permissions to the declared max_scope. The worker's AgentPolicy credential is bound to the delegated scope. This gives CrewAI users cryptographic enforcement of least-privilege delegation — currently they rely on prompt-level instructions that agents can ignore.

title: Standard proof envelope with Content-Type for framework middleware
description: Agent frameworks pass opaque dicts between tools. Define a `application/bolyra-proof+json` content type with a fixed envelope schema: `{version, circuit, publicSignals[], proof{pi_a, pi_b, pi_c}, sessionToken?, delegationChain[]}`. Add serialize/deserialize to both SDKs. This lets LangChain's `StructuredTool` output schema, CrewAI's task output, and AutoGen's message protocol all carry proofs without framework-specific wrappers. Currently each integration reimplements proof serialization differently, which breaks interop when mixing frameworks in a pipeline.

title: LangChain callback handler for automatic auth lifecycle
description: LangChain's callback system (on_tool_start, on_chain_start, on_chain_end) is the natural hook point for auth. Build a `BolyraCallbackHandler` that: (1) on_chain_start triggers a handshake proof if no valid session token exists, (2) on_tool_start checks the tool's required scope against the agent's credential and auto-narrows via delegation if needed, (3) on_chain_end logs the proof artifacts for audit. This is zero-code integration — users add one line: `chain.invoke(input, config={"callbacks": [BolyraCallbackHandler(credential)]})`. The current BolyraAuthTool approach requires explicit tool calls, which breaks the flow for chains that don't know they need auth.

title: Five-line quickstart for each framework
description: The current SDK requires ~30 lines to set up identity, enroll, prove, and verify. Framework users expect copy-paste snippets. Create `bolyra.langchain.authenticate(chain, operator_key)`, `bolyra.crewai.authenticate(crew, operator_key)`, and `bolyra.autogen.authenticate(agent, operator_key)` — each a single function that handles enrollment, tree insertion, and callback/decorator registration internally. Ship these as `bolyra[langchain]`, `bolyra[crewai]`, `bolyra[autogen]` extras in pyproject.toml. Include a working `examples/langchain_auth.py` that runs end-to-end in 5 lines.

title: Define application/bolyra-proof+cbor content type and envelope format
description: The protocol lacks a standardized wire format for proof exchange. Define an IANA-registrable media type application/bolyra-proof+cbor with a CDDL schema covering the three proof types (human, agent, delegation). The envelope should include: proof bytes, public signals array, circuit identifier, proving system tag (groth16/plonk), and a version field. This enables interop between implementations without out-of-band coordination on serialization. Artifact: spec/proof-envelope.cddl + updates to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.md Section 6.

title: Complete DID resolution algorithm with verification method binding
description: spec/did-method-bolyra.md defines did:bolyra but lacks a concrete resolution algorithm mapping on-chain state to DID Documents. Specify: (1) how humanMerkleRoot and agentMerkleRoot map to verification methods, (2) the service endpoint for proof submission, (3) deactivation semantics when a nullifier is revoked. Without this, no universal resolver can implement did:bolyra. Artifact: updated spec/did-method-bolyra.md with Resolution, Deactivation, and Security Considerations sections per W3C DID Core §7-8.

title: Machine-readable conformance test vectors for all three circuits
description: No independent implementation can verify compatibility without standardized test vectors. Produce a spec/test-vectors/ directory with JSON files covering: valid handshake (human+agent), expired credential rejection, stale root rejection, scope subset violation, cumulative bit encoding violation, delegation chain (1-hop and 3-hop), and nonce replay. Each vector includes input signals, expected outputs, and expected pass/fail. This is the minimum bar for any second implementation to claim conformance.

title: Specify human tree root history buffer matching agent tree
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but none for humanTree. The contract comment says 'humanTree uses Semaphore's' but the deployed code uses a standalone LeanIMT with no history. Any new human enrollment immediately invalidates in-flight proofs. Add a parallel ROOT_HISTORY_SIZE buffer for humanTree roots in the registry, and update the IETF draft's Security Considerations to document the staleness window. This is a protocol-level correctness gap, not just an implementation detail.

title: Add blinding salt to scope commitment to prevent bitmask enumeration
description: scopeCommitment = Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment) in AgentPolicy.circom uses only two inputs. Since permissionBitmask is 8 meaningful bits (256 values), an observer can brute-force all possible commitments against a known credentialCommitment to recover the exact permission set. Add a random blinding salt as a third Poseidon input: Poseidon3(bitmask, credCommitment, blindingSalt). The salt becomes a private input; the commitment remains public. Update Delegation.circom to thread the salt through the chain. Document the enumeration attack and mitigation in the IETF draft §9 Security Considerations.

title: Off-chain session token with on-chain checkpoint batching
description: After a successful handshake, emit a signed session token (JWT/SD-JWT with the nullifier, scopeCommitment, and expiry embedded) that parties can verify off-chain for subsequent calls within the session window. Batch session roots on-chain every N minutes. This eliminates per-call proving cost — the single biggest adoption blocker for agentic commerce where agents make dozens of tool calls per minute. Implement in the TS SDK as `createSessionToken()` and `verifySessionToken()`, with a new `SessionCheckpoint.sol` contract that accepts Merkle roots of verified sessions.

title: Payment-channel-aware scope bits for micro-commerce
description: The current FINANCIAL_SMALL/MEDIUM/UNLIMITED permission bits are static. Agentic commerce needs cumulative spending limits enforced across a session — an agent authorized for $100 shouldn't be able to make 50 x $2 calls totaling $100 without the circuit tracking spend. Add a `spentSoFar` private input to AgentPolicy that gets committed alongside the scope, and a `remainingBudget` public output. The on-chain verifier checks remainingBudget >= 0. This turns Bolyra from an auth protocol into a commerce protocol.

title: Verifiable receipt circuit for agent-to-agent commerce
description: When Agent A calls Agent B's tool, there's no cryptographic receipt proving the interaction happened with specific parameters. Add a lightweight ReceiptProof circuit that takes both agents' scopeCommitments, the tool call hash, and a timestamp, producing a receipt commitment. This is the primitive needed for agent expense reporting, audit trails, and dispute resolution — all critical for enterprise adoption of agentic commerce. The `integrations/payment-protocols/` directory already exists but is empty; this gives it purpose.

title: Five-line quickstart for framework-agnostic agent auth
description: The current SDK requires understanding Merkle trees, nonces, and circuits. Ship a high-level `bolyra.protect(toolFunction)` wrapper that handles enrollment, proving, and verification in a single decorator pattern. Target: any LangChain/CrewAI/AutoGen developer adds mutual auth to a tool in 5 lines. Publish as a standalone `@bolyra/agent-auth` package with zero circuit knowledge required. Include an `examples/protected-tool/` that shows a payment tool going from unprotected to Bolyra-protected in a single diff.

title: Delegation chain explorer and SDK introspection API
description: Operators have no way to see who delegated what to whom or trace a scope-narrowing chain after the fact. Add `sdk.traceDelegationChain(sessionNonce)` that reads on-chain DelegationVerified events and reconstructs the chain of scopeCommitments and hop counts. Ship a minimal React component (`@bolyra/chain-explorer`) that visualizes the permission narrowing at each hop. Enterprise buyers won't adopt delegation without audit visibility — this is table stakes for any B2B agentic commerce deployment.

title: Cross-chain Merkle root sync via EIP-1186 storage proofs
description: Deploy IdentityRegistry on Arbitrum and Polygon alongside Base Sepolia, then implement a CrossChainRootRelay contract that accepts EIP-1186 storage proofs to sync humanTree and agentTree roots across chains without a trusted relayer. The relay verifies the source chain's state root against a block hash oracle (e.g., Axiom or the L1 blockhash opcode on L2s) and updates a foreignRoots mapping. This lets a human enrolled on Base prove membership to a verifier on Arbitrum without re-enrolling.

title: Bind sessionNonce to chainId to prevent cross-chain proof replay
description: The current HumanUniqueness and AgentPolicy circuits bind proofs to a sessionNonce but not to a specific chain. A valid proof generated for Base can be replayed on Arbitrum if both chains share the same IdentityRegistry state. Fix by replacing sessionNonce with Poseidon2(sessionNonce, chainId) as the nonce input, and have IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() recompute the binding using block.chainid. This is a 1-line circuit change plus a contract-side hash, but requires re-running trusted setup for affected circuits.

title: Unified root history buffer with chain-of-origin tagging
description: Extend the agentTree's 30-root history buffer pattern to support foreign roots from multiple chains. Add a mapping(uint256 chainId => uint256[30]) foreignAgentRoots and matching foreignHumanRoots to IdentityRegistry, with a pushForeignRoot(chainId, root, storageProof) function gated by the CrossChainRootRelay. The isValidAgentRoot() check then iterates both local and foreign buffers, enabling seamless cross-chain handshake verification without requiring provers to know which chain the counterparty enrolled on.

title: L2 gas cost benchmark matrix for all proof verifications
description: Create a Hardhat script that deploys all three verifier contracts (Human, Agent, Delegation) to forked Base, Arbitrum, and Polygon networks and measures actual gas costs for verifyProof calls across each chain's gas model (Arbitrum's L1 calldata pricing, Polygon's lower base fee, Base's EIP-4844 blob discount). Output a structured JSON report and a markdown comparison table. This informs which chain to recommend as primary deployment and whether PLONK's larger proofs negate its ceremony-free advantage on high-calldata-cost L2s.

title: Cross-chain delegation via CCIP message passing
description: Enable delegation chains that span chains: an agent enrolled on Base delegates to an agent on Arbitrum. Implement a DelegationRelay contract using Chainlink CCIP that takes a verified delegation proof's newScopeCommitment on the source chain and relays it as the previousScopeCommitment input on the destination chain. The relay message includes the sessionNonce, hop count, and destination chain's IdentityRegistry address. The destination contract validates the CCIP message sender and increments the hop counter, preserving the max-3-hop invariant across the full cross-chain path.

title: Add domain separation tags to all nullifier hash computations
description: HumanUniqueness computes nullifier as Poseidon2(scope, secret) and AgentPolicy computes it as Poseidon2(secret, sessionNonce). Both use Poseidon2 with no domain separator, meaning a collision is possible if an agent's (secret, sessionNonce) pair equals a human's (scope, secret) pair — the nullifier spaces overlap. Prepend a unique domain tag constant (e.g., 0xHUMAN, 0xAGENT, 0xDELEG) as the first Poseidon input to each nullifier computation across all three circuits. This is a standard formal verification finding: hash function domain separation prevents cross-protocol nullifier confusion.

title: Prove scope monotonicity invariant holds across full delegation chain
description: The Delegation circuit checks delegateeScope & ~delegatorScope == 0 per hop, but no formal property file proves that this composition is transitive across 3 hops — i.e., that hop3.scope ⊆ hop1.scope always holds given hop3 ⊆ hop2 ⊆ hop1. Write a Certora-style property specification (or circom-witness-based test harness) that exhaustively validates: for all valid 3-hop delegation chains, the final delegatee's permission bitmask is always a subset of the original delegator's. Include counterexample generation for any chain that violates transitivity, especially around the cumulative bit encoding (bit 4 → 2+3).

title: Formal witness injection tests for field-overflow edge cases
description: The circuits use Num2Bits(64) for range checks on permissionBitmask, expiryTimestamp, and currentTimestamp — but modelHash, scope, sessionNonce, and all Merkle siblings have NO range constraints and accept any field element. An attacker could supply modelHash = p + x (where p is the BN254 scalar field order) and get the same credential commitment as modelHash = x. Write a formal test harness that attempts witness generation with inputs at field boundary values (0, 1, p-1, p, 2^253) for every unconstrained signal, and verify the circuit either rejects them or produces the canonical output. Document which signals need Num2Bits guards added.

title: Verify BinaryMerkleRoot depth signal is adequately constrained
description: All three circuits pass merkleProofLength as the depth to BinaryMerkleRoot(MAX_DEPTH). If BinaryMerkleRoot does not internally range-check that depth ≤ MAX_DEPTH, an attacker could supply depth > MAX_DEPTH and either skip sibling checks or trigger undefined behavior in the Mux path. Formally verify that the @zk-kit BinaryMerkleRoot template constrains depth to [1, MAX_DEPTH] and that supplying depth=0 or depth=MAX_DEPTH+1 causes witness generation failure. If the constraint is missing, add an explicit LessThan(log2(MAX_DEPTH)+1) range check on merkleProofLength in each circuit.

title: Close the expiry-liveness gap between circuit and on-chain timestamp
description: AgentPolicy checks expiryTimestamp > currentTimestamp inside the circuit, but currentTimestamp is a public input supplied by the prover. The contract has AgentTimestampStale to bound this, but Delegation.circom also takes currentTimestamp as public input for delegatee expiry — and the on-chain DelegationTimestampStale check must mirror the circuit's LessThan comparison exactly. Write a formal invariant proving that for any (currentTimestamp, expiryTimestamp) pair accepted by the circuit, the on-chain staleness window (block.timestamp ± tolerance) is tight enough that no expired credential can produce a valid proof that passes on-chain verification. Specify the exact tolerance bound and add it to FORMAL-PROPERTIES.md.

title: Structured error code catalog with recovery hints
description: Create a typed error hierarchy in @bolyra/sdk that maps every contract revert (StaleAgentRoot, NonceAlreadyUsed, MaxDelegationHopsExceeded, etc.) and circuit failure (witness generation errors, invalid bitmask) to a structured BolyraError with a numeric code, human-readable message, and a `recovery` string suggesting the fix. Export an ERROR_CODES constant for programmatic matching. Currently developers get raw revert strings or opaque snarkjs exceptions with no guidance on what went wrong or how to fix it.

title: Zero-config quickstart with embedded WASM artifacts
description: Bundle the circuit WASM and verification keys as base64-encoded assets inside @bolyra/sdk so that `createHumanIdentity()` and `proveHandshake()` work without any artifact download or file-path configuration. Add a `bolyra.init()` that lazily loads artifacts on first use. The current quickstart requires pre-built circuit artifacts from a releases page — this is the #1 friction point for new developers trying Bolyra in under 5 minutes.

title: Fluent delegation chain builder API
description: Add a DelegationBuilder class with a chainable API: `Delegation.from(agentCredential).narrowScope(Permission.READ_DATA | Permission.WRITE_DATA).expireBefore(deadline).to(delegateeCredential).prove()`. Internally it validates cumulative-bit invariants, enforces scope narrowing at build time (failing fast with a descriptive error before hitting the circuit), and manages the scopeCommitment chain linking across hops. Currently delegation requires manually constructing witness inputs and tracking previousScopeCommitment between hops.

title: Python SDK with Pydantic models and async prove()
description: Replace the raw subprocess bridge in the Python SDK with Pydantic v2 models for HumanIdentity, AgentCredential, HandshakeProof, and DelegationChain, plus an async `prove_handshake()` that manages the Node subprocess lifecycle transparently. Add `__repr__` and `.to_dict()` for REPL-friendly debugging. LangChain/CrewAI developers expect typed dataclasses and async-first APIs — the current dict-in/dict-out subprocess call feels foreign in Python-native agent frameworks.

title: In-memory mock verifier for unit testing
description: Export a `createMockBolyra()` factory from @bolyra/sdk/testing that returns the full SDK interface but skips circuit compilation and proof generation, returning deterministic mock proofs. Integrators writing tests for their LangChain tools or MCP servers currently must either run the full proving stack (slow, requires circuit artifacts) or hand-roll mocks. A first-party mock with correct type shapes and configurable failure modes (expired credential, stale root) would cut integration test time from minutes to milliseconds.

title: Missing range check on merkleProofLength enables under-constrained tree depth
description: HumanUniqueness and AgentPolicy accept `merkleProofLength` as a private input with no explicit Num2Bits range check. The @zk-kit BinaryMerkleRoot component uses an internal LessThan comparator, but a malicious prover can supply a field element near `r` (the scalar field order) that wraps around the LessThan check, potentially causing the circuit to compute a root over zero siblings (depth=0 means root=leaf). While the on-chain root check is a second barrier, the under-constraint violates the soundness invariant that the prover must demonstrate membership in a tree of the expected structure. Add `component depthRange = Num2Bits(5); depthRange.in <== merkleProofLength;` (capping at MAX_DEPTH=20) to all three circuits.

title: HumanUniqueness nullifier is constant across sessions, enabling cross-verifier linkability
description: The HumanUniqueness nullifier is `Poseidon2(scope, secret)` where scope is a constant like `hash('bolyra-handshake-v1')`. This means every handshake by the same human produces the identical nullifier, allowing colluding verifiers (or a single verifier across sessions) to link all activity to the same pseudonymous identity. The nonceBinding output prevents replay but does NOT prevent linkability. Fix: derive the nullifier as `Poseidon3(scope, secret, sessionNonce)` so each session produces a unique nullifier. Then use a separate `externalNullifier = Poseidon2(scope, secret)` checked on-chain only for revocation/sybil gating, not exposed to the verifier. This preserves sybil resistance while breaking cross-session linkability.

title: scopeCommitment is brute-forceable over the 256-value bitmask space
description: In AgentPolicy, `scopeCommitment = Poseidon2(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment)`. The permissionBitmask is 8-bit (256 possible values, further reduced to ~45 valid values by cumulative encoding rules). An observer who knows the credentialCommitment (public output of a prior AgentPolicy proof, or derivable from enrollment events) can hash all 45 valid bitmask values against it and determine the agent's exact permissions. This breaks the privacy model described in Delegation.circom ('scope bits are PRIVATE'). Fix: add a random blinding salt as a third input: `Poseidon3(bitmask, credCmt, blindingSalt)` where blindingSalt is a private 253-bit random field element. Update Delegation to carry the salt through the chain.

title: currentTimestamp is a trusted public input with no in-circuit freshness bound
description: AgentPolicy accepts `currentTimestamp` as a public input and checks `currentTimestamp < expiryTimestamp` inside the circuit, but there is no constraint binding currentTimestamp to any clock source. The contract-side staleness check (AgentTimestampStale) gates against `block.timestamp`, but a malicious prover can generate a proof with `currentTimestamp = 0` which trivially passes the in-circuit expiry check for any non-zero expiry. If the contract staleness window is too wide (or if proofs are verified off-chain without the contract), expired credentials appear valid. Audit the contract staleness window and document that off-chain verifiers MUST enforce `|currentTimestamp - wallClock| < MAX_SKEW` as a protocol requirement. Add this to the IETF draft security considerations.

title: Delegation token does not bind sessionNonce, enabling cross-session delegation replay
description: The delegation token signed by the delegator is `Poseidon4(delegatorScopeCommitment, delegateeCredCommitment, delegateeScope, delegateeExpiry)`. This token does NOT include `sessionNonce`. A delegator who signs a delegation for session N produces a token that is valid for ANY session — an attacker who intercepts the signed token can present it in a different session as long as the contract's per-session chain state hasn't been initialized. The contract's `DelegationRequiresHandshake` check mitigates this partially, but a race condition exists: if the same human-agent pair performs two handshakes concurrently, the delegation token from session A can be injected into session B's chain. Fix: include `sessionNonce` in the delegation token hash as a 5th Poseidon input and verify it matches the circuit's public `sessionNonce` input.
